questions.question: How do Real-Time Dashboards differ from traditional reports?

options.A: They can only display a single static chart
options.B: They show historical snapshots that never change
options.C: They can be configured to refresh frequently to monitor changing conditions
options.D: They require manual reload before any data updates appear

questions.explanation: Unlike traditional reports that show historical snapshots, Real-Time Dashboards can be configured to refresh frequently, enabling you to monitor changing conditions and respond to current information.

questions.question: What is displayed inside a dashboard tile?

options.A: The results of a KQL query
options.B: A raw connection string
options.D: A list of all dashboard users

questions.explanation: A dashboard contains at least one tile, in which the results of a KQL query are displayed.

questions.question: Under the 'Dashboard editor's identity' authorization option, whose permissions are used to access the data?

options.A: Each viewer's own permissions
options.B: The dashboard creator's permissions
options.D: The workspace member's permissions

questions.explanation: With Dashboard editor's identity, everyone viewing the dashboard accesses data using the dashboard creator's permissions.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of adding multiple pages to a Real-Time Dashboard?

options.A: To increase the auto refresh rate automatically
options.B: To organize related content into logical groups
options.D: To convert tiles into text tiles

questions.explanation: Pages act as containers for more tiles and help you organize related content into logical groups, such as separate pages for different data sources or subject areas.

questions.question: How do you reference a parameter's variable name within a tile query?

options.A: Using an underscore prefix
options.B: Using a dollar sign prefix
options.D: Using an at-sign prefix

questions.explanation: Each parameter has a variable name that you can reference in your tile queries using an underscore prefix.

questions.question: Why might a dashboard editor set a minimum refresh rate?

options.A: To force all viewers to use pass-through identity
options.B: To manage system performance and prevent users from refreshing too frequently
options.D: To add additional pages automatically

questions.explanation: Editors can set a minimum refresh rate to manage system performance and prevent users from refreshing too frequently.

questions.question: In addition to data visualization tiles, what other kind of tile can you add to provide additional information on a dashboard?

options.A: Text tiles
options.B: Pipeline tiles
options.D: Storage tiles

questions.explanation: You can also add text tiles to provide additional information on a dashboard.

questions.question: You are building a Real-Time Dashboard but have not yet created an eventhouse or KQL database. What must you have before you can create the dashboard's data source?

options.A: A Power BI Premium capacity
options.B: A source of real-time data, such as a KQL database in an eventhouse
options.D: A Dataflow Gen2

questions.explanation: To create a Real-Time Dashboard you need a source of real-time data, such as a KQL database in an eventhouse, which the dashboard's data source references.

questions.question: Your organization requires that each dashboard viewer only see data they are individually authorized to access at the source. Which data source authorization option should you configure?

options.A: Dashboard editor's identity
options.B: Pass-through identity
options.D: Anonymous access

questions.explanation: Pass-through identity ensures each person viewing the dashboard accesses data using their own permissions, enforcing per-viewer authorization.

questions.question: A team lead wants all dashboard viewers to see the same complete dataset regardless of their individual source permissions, relying on the creator's access. Which authorization option meets this requirement?

options.A: Pass-through identity
options.B: Dashboard editor's identity
options.D: Capacity admin identity

questions.explanation: With Dashboard editor's identity, everyone viewing the dashboard accesses data using the dashboard creator's permissions, so all viewers see the same data.

questions.question: A dashboard has grown to include tiles for bike availability, dock capacity, weather, and revenue. The editor wants to keep weather-related tiles separate from operational tiles for clarity. What is the best approach using built-in features?

options.A: Create separate dashboards for each subject
options.B: Add multiple pages and group related tiles by subject area
options.C: Convert the weather tiles into text tiles
options.D: Lower the auto refresh rate for weather tiles

questions.explanation: You can add more pages as containers for tiles to organize related content into logical groups, such as separate pages for different subject areas.

questions.question: Viewers of a bike rental dashboard want to focus on only one or two specific neighborhoods at a time without the editor creating a separate tile for each. What feature should the editor implement?

options.A: A base query
options.B: A parameter that lets users filter by neighborhood
options.D: A minimum refresh rate

questions.explanation: Parameters let users filter the data displayed in tiles; a neighborhood parameter allows viewers to focus on a particular subset of the data interactively.

questions.question: An editor created a parameter named selected_neighbourhoods to filter tiles. When writing the tile query, how must the parameter be referenced?

options.A: As selected_neighbourhoods with no prefix
options.B: As _selected_neighbourhoods with an underscore prefix
options.D: As {{selected_neighbourhoods}}

questions.explanation: Parameters are referenced in tile queries using an underscore prefix, so the variable would be referenced as _selected_neighbourhoods.

questions.question: During peak hours, several viewers keep manually refreshing a dashboard, putting load on the system. The editor wants viewers to be able to adjust refresh during their session but not refresh too aggressively. What should the editor configure?

options.A: Disable auto refresh entirely
options.B: Set a default refresh rate and a minimum refresh rate
options.D: Remove all parameters from the dashboard

questions.explanation: Editors can set a default refresh rate and viewers can adjust it during their session, while a minimum refresh rate prevents refreshing too frequently to manage system performance.

questions.question: A dashboard has five tiles that all start by retrieving the latest observation per neighborhood within the last 30 minutes, then each tile filters or projects differently. The editor wants to reduce duplicated query logic. What is the recommended approach?

options.A: Copy the query text into every tile manually
options.B: Define a base query and reference it from each tile
options.C: Create a separate KQL database per tile
options.D: Convert each tile into a text tile

questions.explanation: Base queries retrieve a general set of records relevant for multiple tiles; tile-specific queries then filter or group that data, avoiding duplicated logic across tiles.

questions.question: An editor defines a base query and assigns it the variable name _base_bike_data. How would an individual tile query use this base query?

options.A: By copying the base query's full text into the tile
options.B: By starting the tile query with _base_bike_data and then adding project/order operators
options.C: By naming the tile the same as the base query
options.D: By enabling auto refresh on the tile

questions.explanation: Tile queries reference the base query by its variable name, for example starting with _base_bike_data and then applying project and order by operators for the specific visualization.

questions.question: A viewer selects no neighborhoods in the neighborhood parameter control. Given a tile query that uses isempty(_selected_neighbourhoods) or Neighbourhood in (_selected_neighbourhoods), what data is shown?

options.A: No data at all
options.B: Only the first neighborhood alphabetically
options.D: An error is returned

questions.explanation: The filter checks whether the parameter is empty (no neighborhoods selected) and, if so, shows all neighborhoods; otherwise it shows only the selected ones.

questions.question: An analyst wants a tile that shows only the most recent record ingested for each neighborhood in the bikes table. Which KQL pattern from the dashboard examples achieves this?

options.A: summarize count() by Neighbourhood
options.B: summarize latest_observation = arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood
options.D: top 1 by Neighbourhood

questions.explanation: The example uses summarize latest_observation = arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood to retrieve the most recent observation (latest record ingested) for each neighborhood.

questions.question: A dashboard tile must show data only from the last 30 minutes of ingested bike records. Which KQL clause from the examples implements this time window?

options.A: where ingestion_time() between (ago(30min) .. now())
options.B: where Timestamp > 30
options.D: limit 30min

questions.explanation: The dashboard examples use where ingestion_time() between (ago(30min) .. now()) to restrict records to those ingested within the last 30 minutes.

questions.question: An editor wants tile results ordered so neighborhoods appear alphabetically from A to Z in the visualization. Which operator and direction should be appended to the tile query?

options.A: order by Neighbourhood desc
options.B: order by Neighbourhood asc
options.D: top 10 by Neighbourhood

questions.explanation: The example tile query ends with order by Neighbourhood asc to display neighborhoods in ascending (A to Z) order.

questions.question: A stakeholder asks whether viewers can change how often a dashboard updates after the editor has set it up. Based on the auto refresh behavior, what is true?

options.A: Only the editor can ever change the refresh rate
options.B: Viewers can adjust the refresh rate during their session, bounded by the editor's minimum rate
options.C: Refresh rate cannot be changed once published
options.D: Viewers can disable the editor's minimum refresh rate

questions.explanation: Editors set a default refresh rate, viewers can adjust this rate during their session, and the editor's minimum refresh rate prevents refreshing too frequently.

questions.question: When you first add a data visualization tile to a dashboard, what should you do before the visualization is finalized?

options.A: Publish the dashboard immediately
options.B: Enter and test the KQL query you want to use against the data source
options.C: Set the minimum refresh rate to zero
options.D: Convert it to a text tile

questions.explanation: When you first add a tile, you can enter and test the query you want to use to query the underlying data source before visualizing the results.

questions.question: A retail operations team uses an eventhouse fed by streaming POS events. They want a dashboard where one parameter filters by store region and the same filter applies consistently to several tiles. What design lets a single parameter influence multiple tiles?

options.A: Hard-code the region in each tile query
options.B: Create one dashboard parameter and reference its underscore-prefixed variable in each tile query
options.D: Use a separate dashboard per region

questions.explanation: A single dashboard parameter can be referenced in multiple tile queries using its underscore-prefixed variable name, so changing it filters all those tiles consistently.

questions.question: A dashboard editor combines a base query and a parameter. The base query returns the latest observation per neighborhood, and a parameter filters neighborhoods. What is the correct relationship between them in tile design?

options.A: Parameters cannot be used together with base queries
options.B: Tile queries reference the base query variable and apply the parameter filter to refine results
options.C: Base queries override all parameter selections
options.D: The parameter must be defined inside the base query's variable name

questions.explanation: Base queries provide shared general records referenced by variable name, while parameters add interactive filtering; tile queries can reference the base query and incorporate parameter-based filters to refine what is shown.

questions.question: An organization wants to ensure that even if a careless viewer tries, the dashboard cannot be refreshed faster than every 30 seconds, while still allowing viewers some control. Which combination of settings achieves this?

options.A: Disable auto refresh and set pass-through identity
options.B: Set a default refresh rate and a minimum refresh rate of 30 seconds
options.C: Remove auto refresh and rely on manual reload
options.D: Set the editor identity and add more pages

questions.explanation: Setting a default refresh rate gives a baseline, while a minimum refresh rate prevents viewers from refreshing more frequently than allowed, managing system performance.

questions.question: CityBikes operates a bike-sharing service across many neighborhoods. Streaming telemetry (bikes available, empty docks, ingestion time) flows into a KQL database in an eventhouse. The operations team needs a Real-Time Dashboard that: (1) shows the latest observation per neighborhood from the last 30 minutes, (2) lets operators interactively filter to specific neighborhoods, (3) groups operational tiles separately from a planned revenue section, (4) avoids duplicating identical query logic across five operational tiles, (5) ensures each operator only sees neighborhoods they are authorized for, and (6) prevents viewers from hammering the system with overly frequent refreshes. Which combination of Fabric Real-Time Dashboard features correctly satisfies ALL six requirements?

options.A: A single static page with five copied queries; editor's identity; no parameters; auto refresh with no minimum; manual filtering
options.B: A base query (e.g., _base_bike_data) referenced by the five tiles; a neighborhood parameter referenced as _selected_neighbourhoods; multiple pages to separate operations and revenue; pass-through identity for per-operator authorization; a default plus minimum refresh rate
options.C: Pass-through identity; one page; five independent KQL databases; no base query; no minimum refresh rate; a text tile for filtering
options.D: Editor's identity; parameters defined with an @ prefix; a single page; auto refresh disabled; one giant tile showing all neighborhoods

questions.explanation: A base query referenced by multiple tiles avoids duplicated logic (req 4); a neighborhood parameter referenced with the underscore prefix enables interactive filtering (req 2) and supports the latest-observation-per-neighborhood pattern over the 30-minute window (req 1); multiple pages group operational versus revenue tiles (req 3); pass-through identity enforces per-operator authorization (req 5); and setting a default plus minimum refresh rate manages performance while allowing viewer adjustment (req 6). Option B is the only choice that uses each feature correctly.

questions.question:
Consider the following dashboard tile query:

bikes
| where ingestion_time() between (ago(30min) .. now())
| summarize latest_observation = arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood
| project Neighbourhood, latest_observation, No_Bikes, No_Empty_Docks
| order by Neighbourhood asc

What does this query return?

options.A: All bike records from the entire history of the table
options.B: One row per neighborhood with its most recent observation from the last 30 minutes, ordered alphabetically
options.C: A count of bikes per neighborhood with no time filter
options.D: Only neighborhoods that currently have zero empty docks

questions.explanation: The where clause limits to the last 30 minutes, arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood returns the latest record per neighborhood, project selects key columns, and order by Neighbourhood asc sorts alphabetically.

questions.question:
A dashboard parameter named _selected_neighbourhoods drives this tile query:

bikes
| where ingestion_time() between (ago(30min) .. now())
    and (isempty(_selected_neighbourhoods) or Neighbourhood in (_selected_neighbourhoods))
| summarize latest_observation = arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood

If a viewer selects 'Downtown' and 'Harbor', which rows appear?

options.A: All neighborhoods, because the parameter is ignored
options.B: Only the latest observations for Downtown and Harbor within the last 30 minutes
options.C: Downtown and Harbor records from all time
options.D: No rows, because two values cannot be passed

questions.explanation: Since the parameter is not empty, the isempty branch is false and Neighbourhood in (_selected_neighbourhoods) restricts to Downtown and Harbor; the time filter limits to the last 30 minutes and arg_max returns each one's latest observation.

questions.question:
Given a base query defined with variable name _base_bike_data:

// Base query
bikes
| where ingestion_time() between (ago(30min) .. now())
| summarize latest_observation = arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood

Which tile query correctly reuses the base query to project key columns sorted alphabetically?

options.A: bikes | project Neighbourhood, No_Bikes | order by Neighbourhood asc
options.B: _base_bike_data | project Neighbourhood, latest_observation, No_Bikes, No_Empty_Docks | order by Neighbourhood asc
options.D: summarize _base_bike_data by Neighbourhood

questions.explanation: Tile queries reference the base query by its variable name, so the tile begins with _base_bike_data and then applies project and order by operators to shape the visualization.

questions.question: An editor wants a tile that shows, for each neighborhood, the number of available bikes from the most recent observation, but only for neighborhoods where fewer than 3 bikes remain (low-supply alert). Building on the latest-observation pattern, which query is correct?

options.A: bikes | summarize arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood | where No_Bikes < 3 | project Neighbourhood, No_Bikes
options.B: bikes | where No_Bikes < 3 | take 1
options.C: bikes | project No_Bikes | order by No_Bikes desc
options.D: bikes | summarize count() by No_Bikes

questions.explanation: First reduce to the latest observation per neighborhood with arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood, then apply where No_Bikes < 3 to keep only low-supply neighborhoods, and project the relevant columns. The other options do not compute the latest per-neighborhood value or filter correctly.

questions.question: A parameter _time_window is defined to let users choose a lookback duration (for example, a timespan value). Which tile query correctly applies it to the ingestion-time filter, consistent with the dashboard parameter pattern?

options.A: bikes | where ingestion_time() between (ago(_time_window) .. now()) | summarize arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood
options.B: bikes | where _time_window = 30min
options.D: bikes | where ingestion_time() > _time_window ago

questions.explanation: Parameters are referenced by their underscore-prefixed variable name inside the query, so ago(_time_window) substitutes the user-selected timespan into the time-window filter while keeping the latest-observation summarization.

questions.question:
An editor writes the following filter expression in a tile query to support an optional multi-select parameter:

| where isempty(_selected_neighbourhoods) or Neighbourhood in (_selected_neighbourhoods)

What is the design intent of including the isempty() check?

options.A: To raise an error when no value is selected
options.B: To show all neighborhoods when nothing is selected and only chosen ones when values are provided
options.C: To permanently default the dashboard to a single neighborhood
options.D: To disable auto refresh while the parameter is empty

questions.explanation: The isempty() branch makes the filter pass-through (show all neighborhoods) when no selection exists; otherwise the in() clause restricts results to the selected neighborhoods.

questions.question: An editor wants a tile that visualizes total available bikes across the whole city from the latest observations. Building on the per-neighborhood latest pattern, which query produces a single citywide total?

options.A: bikes | where ingestion_time() between (ago(30min) .. now()) | summarize arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood | summarize TotalBikes = sum(No_Bikes)
options.B: bikes | summarize sum(No_Bikes) by ingestion_time()
options.D: bikes | order by No_Bikes desc | take 1

questions.explanation: First obtain the latest observation per neighborhood with arg_max over the 30-minute window, then a second summarize with sum(No_Bikes) aggregates those per-neighborhood latest values into one citywide total. The other options do not first reduce to the latest per neighborhood before summing.

questions.question: Two tiles share identical leading logic that returns the latest observation per neighborhood over the last 30 minutes. To follow the dashboard maintainability guidance, how should this be structured in KQL across the base query and tiles?

options.A: Repeat the full where/summarize logic verbatim in both tiles
options.B: Place the shared where/summarize in a base query named _base_bike_data and have each tile start from _base_bike_data, then add tile-specific project or summarize operators
options.C: Move the logic into the parameter definition
options.D: Create two separate KQL databases, one per tile

questions.explanation: The guidance is to define a base query holding the shared general logic and reference it by its variable name (_base_bike_data) from each tile, where tile-specific queries then filter, project, or group the data, avoiding duplication.

questions.question:
Given the tile query below, which line is responsible for collapsing multiple ingested rows per neighborhood down to just the single most recent one?

bikes
| where ingestion_time() between (ago(30min) .. now())   // line 1
| summarize latest_observation = arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood   // line 2
| project Neighbourhood, No_Bikes, No_Empty_Docks   // line 3
| order by Neighbourhood asc   // line 4

options.A: Line 1
options.B: Line 2
options.D: Line 4

questions.explanation: Line 2 uses summarize with arg_max(ingestion_time(), *) by Neighbourhood, which returns the row having the maximum ingestion time per neighborhood, collapsing multiple rows to the single most recent observation. Line 1 filters time, line 3 selects columns, and line 4 sorts.
